examples.title: Sample Mean Calculation and Parameter Estimation
examples.description: Demonstrates calculating sample statistics to estimate population parameters
examples.code:
import statistics

# Sample data (e.g., heights of 50 students in cm)
sample_data = [165, 170, 168, 172, 175, 160, 158, 162, 169, 174,
              171, 167, 163, 176, 159, 164, 173, 168, 170, 166,
              161, 177, 169, 165, 171, 167, 172, 164, 170, 168,
              175, 163, 169, 166, 171, 174, 162, 168, 170, 165,
              173, 167, 164, 171, 169, 175, 163, 170, 168, 172]

# Calculate sample statistics
sample_mean = statistics.mean(sample_data)
sample_std = statistics.stdev(sample_data)
sample_variance = statistics.variance(sample_data)

# Population parameter estimates (point estimates)
print(f"Sample Size: {len(sample_data)}")
print(f"Sample Mean (x̄): {sample_mean:.2f} cm")
print(f"Sample Std Dev (s): {sample_std:.2f} cm")
print(f"Sample Variance (s²): {sample_variance:.2f}")
print(f"\nPoint Estimates:")
print(f"  Population Mean (μ) ≈ {sample_mean:.2f} cm")
print(f"  Population Std Dev (σ) ≈ {sample_std:.2f} cm")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the fundamental concept of statistical inference: using sample statistics (x̄, s) to estimate population parameters (μ, σ). The sample mean serves as the best point estimate for the unknown population mean.

examples.title: Confidence Interval Calculation for Population Mean
examples.description: Demonstrates interval estimation using sample data
examples.code:
import math
import statistics

def confidence_interval(data, confidence=0.95):
    """Calculate confidence interval for population mean."""
    n = len(data)
    mean = statistics.mean(data)
    std_err = statistics.stdev(data) / math.sqrt(n)
    
    # Critical value from t-distribution (approximation using 1.96 for large n)
    if n >= 30:  # Use z-value for large samples
        z_score = 1.96  # For 95% confidence
    else:  # Use t-value for small samples (approximation)
        z_score = 2.0
    
    margin_of_error = z_score * std_err
    
    return (mean - margin_of_error, mean + margin_of_error)

# Sample data
sample_data = [23, 25, 28, 24, 27, 26, 22, 29, 25, 24,
              26, 28, 27, 25, 26, 24, 23, 28, 27, 25]

lower, upper = confidence_interval(sample_data)

print(f"Sample Size: {len(sample_data)}")
print(f"Sample Mean: {statistics.mean(sample_data):.2f}")
print(f"95% Confidence Interval: ({lower:.2f}, {upper:.2f})")
print(f"Interpretation: We are 95% confident that the true")
print(f"population mean lies between {lower:.2f} and {upper:.2f}")
examples.explanation: This demonstrates interval estimation, a key concept in statistical inference. Instead of a single point estimate, we provide a range (confidence interval) within which the true population parameter is likely to fall with a specified probability.

examples.title: Hypothesis Testing: Z-Test for Population Mean
examples.description: Demonstrates hypothesis testing for large samples
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates hypothesis testing, a core component of statistical inference. It performs a z-test to determine whether there is sufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis that the population mean equals a specified value.

examples.title: Random Sampling Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates random sampling concept and sampling distribution
examples.code:
import random
import statistics

# Create a population of 10,000 values (e.g., exam scores)
population = [random.randint(40, 100) for _ in range(10000)]
true_population_mean = statistics.mean(population)
print(f"Population Size: {len(population)}")
print(f"True Population Mean (μ): {true_population_mean:.2f}")
print("="*50)

# Simulate taking multiple samples
def take_sample(population, sample_size):
    """Take a random sample from population."""
    return random.sample(population, sample_size)

# Take 1000 samples, each of size 30
num_samples = 1000
sample_size = 30
sample_means = []

for i in range(num_samples):
    sample = take_sample(population, sample_size)
    sample_means.append(statistics.mean(sample))

# Analyze the sampling distribution
print(f"\n=== Sampling Distribution Analysis ===")
print(f"Number of Samples: {num_samples}")
print(f"Sample Size: {sample_size}")
print(f"Mean of Sample Means: {statistics.mean(sample_means):.2f}")
print(f"Std Dev of Sample Means: {statistics.stdev(sample_means):.2f}")
print(f"\nTheoretical Standard Error (σ/√n): {statistics.stdev(population)/sample_size:.2f}")
print("\nObservation: Mean of sample means ≈ True population mean")
print("(This demonstrates the property of unbiased estimators)")
examples.explanation: This simulation demonstrates key concepts of statistical inference: random sampling, sampling distribution, and the relationship between sample statistics and population parameters. It shows that as we take many samples, the distribution of sample means centers around the true population mean.
